cap-outpatient-healthy.title: CAP — Outpatient, no comorbidities, no MDR risk

regimen.dose_text: Amoxicillin 1 g PO TID
Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID (alternative)
Azithromycin 500 mg PO ×1 then 250 mg PO daily ×4d (only if local pneumococcal macrolide resistance <25%)
regimen.caveat: Macrolide resistance >25% in most US regions; reserve for true exceptions

cap-outpatient-healthy.duration: 5 days minimum; longer if not clinically improved

cap-outpatient-comorbid.title: CAP — Outpatient with comorbidities (chronic heart/lung/liver/renal disease, DM, ETOH, malignancy, asplenia)

regimen.dose_text: Amox-clav 875/125 mg PO BID + doxycycline 100 mg PO BID
Amox-clav 875/125 mg PO BID + azithromycin 500 mg ×1 then 250 mg daily
Levofloxacin 750 mg PO daily (monotherapy alternative)
Ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV daily + azithromycin 500 mg IV/PO daily
Levofloxacin 750 mg IV/PO daily (monotherapy)
Ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily + azithromycin 500 mg IV daily
Ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily + levofloxacin 750 mg IV daily

cap-mrsa-pseudo-risk.title: CAP — Empiric MRSA or Pseudomonas coverage (only if individual + local risk factors)

regimen.dose_text: Vancomycin AUC-targeted (use vancomyzer.com) — add only if MRSA risk factors present
Linezolid 600 mg IV/PO BID (alternative MRSA agent)
Pip-tazo 4.5 g IV q6h (extended infusion preferred) — add only if Pseudomonas risk factors
Cefepime 2 g IV q8h — alternative anti-pseudomonal

cap-mrsa-pseudo-risk.caveat: Per 2023 pathway: empiric MRSA AND Pseudomonas coverage should NOT be started together unless risk factors for both.

regimen.dose_text: Vancomycin (AUC-targeted) + pip-tazo 4.5 g IV q6h (extended infusion)
Vancomycin (AUC-targeted) + cefepime 2 g IV q8h
Linezolid 600 mg IV BID + meropenem 1 g IV q8h (extended infusion 3h)
Amox-clav 875/125 mg PO BID
Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID
Azithromycin 500 mg ×1 then 250 mg daily ×4d

cystitis-first-line.title: Acute uncomplicated cystitis — First-line

regimen.dose_text: Nitrofurantoin macrocrystals 100 mg PO BID × 5 days
regimen.caveat: Avoid if CrCl <30 mL/min (efficacy concerns); avoid in pyelonephritis

regimen.dose_text: TMP-SMX DS (160/800 mg) 1 tab PO BID × 3 days
regimen.caveat: Avoid if local E. coli resistance >20%

regimen.dose_text: Fosfomycin 3 g PO ×1
Pivmecillinam 185 mg PO TID × 3-7 days (Pivya, FDA 2024)

cystitis-first-line.source: IDSA Uncomplicated UTI 2010 + 2024 FDA Pivya approval

cystitis-alternatives.title: Cystitis — Alternatives / when first-line not appropriate

regimen.dose_text: Ciprofloxacin 250 mg PO BID × 3 days
regimen.caveat: Reserve due to FQ collateral damage / BBW

regimen.dose_text: Levofloxacin 250 mg PO daily × 3 days
Amox-clav 500/125 mg PO BID × 5-7 days (less effective)
Gepotidacin (Blujepa) 1500 mg PO BID × 5 days (FDA 2025; female ≥12y, ≥40 kg)
Sulopenem etzadroxil/probenecid (Orlynvah) 500/500 mg PO BID × 5 days (FDA 2024; ESBL/FQ-resistant)
Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BID × 7 days
Levofloxacin 750 mg PO daily × 5 days
TMP-SMX DS 1 tab PO BID × 14 days (only if susceptible; consider initial IV/IM long-acting agent)

pyelo-inpatient.title: Pyelonephritis — Inpatient

regimen.dose_text: Ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily
Pip-tazo 3.375 g IV q6h (if MDR risk)
Ertapenem 1 g IV daily (ESBL or beta-lactam allergy)

pyelo-inpatient.duration: Total 7-14 days; transition PO when clinically improved

cuti-empiric.title: Complicated UTI / CAUTI — Empiric (no known MDR)

regimen.dose_text: Ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV daily
Pip-tazo 3.375-4.5 g IV q6h
Cefepime 2 g IV q8h (if Pseudomonas risk)

cuti-empiric.duration: 7 days if rapid response; 10-14 days if delayed

regimen.dose_text: TMP-SMX 5 mg/kg (TMP) IV/PO BID — preferred if susceptible
Cipro 400 mg IV q12h or 500-750 mg PO BID — preferred if susceptible
Ertapenem 1 g IV daily — preferred when FQ/SMX not options
Meropenem 1 g IV q8h — for severely ill
Cephalexin 500 mg PO QID
Dicloxacillin 500 mg PO QID
Clindamycin 300-450 mg PO QID (PCN allergy)
Cefazolin 1-2 g IV q8h
Nafcillin 1-2 g IV q4-6h (MSSA)
Clindamycin 600 mg IV q8h (PCN allergy)
Incision and drainage alone for simple abscess (procedural — antibiotics are adjunctive)
TMP-SMX DS 1-2 tabs PO BID
Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID
Clindamycin 300-450 mg PO QID
Vancomycin AUC-targeted (vancomyzer.com)
Linezolid 600 mg IV/PO BID
Daptomycin 6 mg/kg IV daily (NOT pneumonia)

nec-fasc-empiric.title: Necrotizing fasciitis — Empiric

regimen.dose_text: Vancomycin + pip-tazo 4.5 g IV q6h + clindamycin 900 mg IV q8h (toxin suppression)
Meropenem 1 g IV q8h + vancomycin + clindamycin (severe/MDR)

nec-fasc-empiric.caveat: Surgical debridement is the most important intervention. Antibiotics alone insufficient.

dfi-mild.title: Diabetic foot — Mild (outpatient, no bone, MRSA risk-stratified)

regimen.dose_text: Cephalexin 500 mg PO QID
Amox-clav 875/125 mg PO BID
TMP-SMX DS BID + amoxicillin (MRSA + strep coverage)
Ampicillin-sulbactam 3 g IV q6h
Ertapenem 1 g IV daily
Ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV daily + metronidazole 500 mg IV/PO q8h

dfi-severe.title: Diabetic foot — Severe / limb-threatening

regimen.dose_text: Vancomycin + pip-tazo 4.5 g IV q6h
Vancomycin + meropenem 1 g IV q8h (if ESBL/MDR risk)
Daptomycin 6 mg/kg + pip-tazo (vanc-intolerant)

dfi-severe.duration: Soft tissue 2-4 wk; osteo 4-6 wk if no resection

osteo-empiric.title: Osteomyelitis — Empiric (pending culture)

regimen.dose_text: Vancomycin + ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily
Vancomycin + cefepime 2 g IV q8h (Pseudomonas risk)

osteo-empiric.duration: Hold antibiotics for biopsy when feasible

regimen.dose_text: Nafcillin 2 g IV q4h
Cefazolin 2 g IV q8h
Vancomycin AUC-targeted (vancomyzer.com)
Daptomycin 6-8 mg/kg IV daily
Vancomycin + ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV daily
Vancomycin + ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h
Nafcillin 2 g IV q4h × 6 weeks
Cefazolin 2 g IV q8h × 6 weeks
Vancomycin AUC-targeted × 6 weeks
Daptomycin 8-10 mg/kg IV daily × 6 weeks

ie-vgs.title: Native-valve IE — Viridans strep (PCN MIC ≤0.12)

regimen.dose_text: Aqueous PCN G 12-18 MU/24h IV continuous or divided q4h × 4 weeks
Ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily × 4 weeks

ie-vgs.duration: 4 weeks (2 wk if combo with gent in selected pts)

ie-enterococcus.title: Native-valve IE — E. faecalis

regimen.dose_text: Ampicillin 2 g IV q4h + ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h × 6 weeks
Ampicillin 2 g IV q4h + gentamicin 3 mg/kg/d divided q8h × 4-6 wk

ie-enterococcus.duration: 6 weeks (4 wk if symptoms <3mo and native valve)

meningitis-empiric-adult.title: Bacterial meningitis — Adult 18-50, community

regimen.dose_text: Vancomycin AUC-targeted + ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h
Add dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV q6h × 4 days, ideally before/with first dose (adjunctive anti-inflammatory — not an antibiotic)

meningitis-empiric-adult.duration: Tailor to organism (S. pneumo 10-14d, N. mening 7d)

meningitis-empiric-elderly.title: Bacterial meningitis — Adult >50 or immunocompromised (cover Listeria)

regimen.dose_text: Vancomycin + ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h + ampicillin 2 g IV q4h
+ Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV q6h × 4d (adjunctive anti-inflammatory — not an antibiotic)
Vancomycin + cefepime 2 g IV q8h
Vancomycin + meropenem 2 g IV q8h
Ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV daily + metronidazole 500 mg IV/PO q8h
Ertapenem 1 g IV daily
Amp-sulbactam — NOT recommended (high E. coli resistance)
Pip-tazo 4.5 g IV q6h
Meropenem 1 g IV q8h
Cefepime 2 g IV q8h + metronidazole 500 mg IV q8h

iai-severe.title: Severe IAI / septic shock — broad with MDR coverage

regimen.dose_text: Meropenem 1 g IV q8h + vancomycin (if MRSA/VRE risk)
Emblaveo 6 g loading then 1.5 g IV q6h (cIAI w/ MBL CRE — FDA 2025)
Fidaxomicin 200 mg PO BID × 10 days (preferred)
Vancomycin 125 mg PO QID × 10 days (alternative)
Fidaxomicin 200 mg PO BID × 10 days OR extended-pulse 200 mg BID days 1-5 then once every other day days 7-25
Vancomycin tapered/pulsed regimen
+ Bezlotoxumab 10 mg/kg IV ×1 (recurrence prevention — adjunctive monoclonal antibody, not an antibiotic)
Vancomycin 500 mg PO/NG QID + metronidazole 500 mg IV q8h; consider rectal vanco if ileus
Cefepime 2 g IV q8h (monotherapy)
Pip-tazo 4.5 g IV q6h
Meropenem 1 g IV q8h (if ESBL or recent broad-spectrum exposure)
+ Vancomycin only if specific indication (CR-BSI, soft tissue, MRSA colonization, hemodynamic instability)

sepsis-empiric.title: Septic shock — Broad empiric while source unclear

regimen.dose_text: Vancomycin + pip-tazo 4.5 g IV q6h
Vancomycin + meropenem 1 g IV q8h (if pip-tazo allergy or MDR risk)
Amoxicillin 1000 mg PO daily OR 500 mg PO BID × 10 days (peds: 50 mg/kg/d max 1000 mg)
PCN VK 500 mg PO BID-TID × 10 days
Cephalexin 500 mg PO BID × 10 days (PCN allergy, non-anaphylactic)
Amox-clav 875/125 mg PO BID (high-dose 2000/125 ER if risk factors)
Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID (PCN allergy)

aom-peds.title: Acute otitis media (pediatric)

regimen.dose_text: Amoxicillin 80-90 mg/kg/d divided BID (max 4 g/d)
Amox-clav 90/6.4 mg/kg/d divided BID (recent abx, recurrent, conjunctivitis)

aom-peds.duration: 10 days <2y or severe; 5-7d if older with mild disease

regimen.dose_text: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM ×1 (1 g IM if ≥150 kg)
+ Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID × 7d if chlamydia not excluded
Zoliflodacin 3 g PO ×1 (FDA 2025; alternative for ceftriaxone-allergic)
Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID × 7 days (preferred)
Azithromycin 1 g PO ×1 (alternative; pregnancy)
Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM ×1 + doxycycline 100 mg PO BID × 14d + metronidazole 500 mg PO BID × 14d
Early (primary, secondary, early latent): Bicillin LA 2.4 MU IM ×1
Late latent / unknown duration / tertiary (non-CNS): Bicillin LA 2.4 MU IM weekly ×3
Neurosyphilis: Aqueous PCN G 18-24 MU/d IV continuous or 3-4 MU IV q4h × 10-14 days
Nitrofurantoin 100 mg PO BID × 5d
TMP-SMX DS BID × 3-5d
Ciprofloxacin 250 mg PO BID × 3d
Fosfomycin 3 g PO ×1 (E. coli only; not preferred for Klebsiella)
TMP-SMX 5 mg/kg (TMP) IV/PO BID
Cipro 400 mg IV q12h or 500-750 mg PO BID
Levofloxacin 750 mg IV/PO daily
Ertapenem 1 g IV daily — when FQ/SMX not options
Meropenem 1 g IV q8h (preferred)
Ertapenem 1 g IV daily — for non-critically ill, source-controlled
Meropenem-vaborbactam 4 g IV q8h
Ceftazidime-avibactam 2.5 g IV q8h
Imipenem-relebactam 1.25 g IV q6h
Aztreonam-avibactam (Emblaveo, 2025) 6 g IV load then 1.5 g IV q6h
Ceftaz-avi 2.5 g IV q8h + aztreonam 2 g IV q6-8h (legacy combination)
Cefiderocol 2 g IV q8h
Ceftazidime-avibactam 2.5 g IV q8h
Cefiderocol 2 g IV q8h (alternative)

pseudomonas-treatment.title: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (susceptible)

regimen.dose_text: Pip-tazo 4.5 g IV q6h (extended infusion over 4h preferred)
Cefepime 2 g IV q8h
Ceftazidime 2 g IV q8h
Aztreonam 2 g IV q8h (preferred for IgE-mediated PCN/cephalosporin allergy)
Meropenem 1-2 g IV q8h (reserve; use only when other agents not viable)
Ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV q8h or 750 mg PO BID (most active FQ vs. Pseudomonas)
Tobramycin 5-7 mg/kg IV q24h (UTI or combination; not monotherapy for systemic)
Amikacin 15-20 mg/kg IV q24h (UTI or combination; reserve for tobramycin-resistant)

pseudomonas-treatment.caveat: Monotherapy preferred for susceptible strains (non-neutropenic). Reserve carbapenems to preserve activity. Aminoglycosides as monotherapy only for UTI or combination adjunct.

pseudomonas-dtr.title: DTR-Pseudomonas (difficult-to-treat resistant)

regimen.dose_text: Ceftolozane-tazobactam 1.5-3 g IV q8h (preferred if susceptible)
Ceftazidime-avibactam 2.5 g IV q8h
Imipenem-relebactam 1.25 g IV q6h
Aztreonam-avibactam 6.5 g IV q8h (MBL-producing strains; NDM, VIM, IMP)
Cefiderocol 2 g IV q8h (pan-DTR, last resort)

pseudomonas-dtr.caveat: Aztreonam-avibactam is the agent of choice for MBL-producing DTR-Pseudomonas. Susceptibility testing required before selecting agent.

regimen.dose_text: Sulbactam-durlobactam (Xacduro, 2023) 1 g + 1 g IV q6h (3h infusion)
High-dose ampicillin-sulbactam 9-12 g/day sulbactam component (e.g., 27 g/d amp-sulb continuous or divided)
Minocycline 200 mg IV/PO q12h (combination)
TMP-SMX (10-15 mg/kg/d TMP) + minocycline 200 mg IV/PO q12h
TMP-SMX + cefiderocol 2 g IV q8h
Minocycline + tigecycline (TMP-SMX-intolerant)

mssa-bacteremia.title: MSSA bacteremia

regimen.dose_text: Nafcillin 2 g IV q4h
Cefazolin 2 g IV q8h

mssa-bacteremia.duration: ≥14 days uncomplicated; longer if endocarditis or metastatic foci

mrsa-bacteremia.title: MRSA bacteremia

regimen.dose_text: Vancomycin AUC-targeted (vancomyzer.com)
Daptomycin 8-10 mg/kg IV daily (NOT pneumonia)

mrsa-bacteremia.duration: ≥14 days uncomplicated; longer if endocarditis or metastatic foci

regimen.dose_text: TMP-SMX DS 1-2 tabs PO BID
Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID
Clindamycin 300-450 mg PO QID
Linezolid 600 mg PO BID (severe/IV)
Vancomycin AUC-targeted
Linezolid 600 mg IV/PO BID
Daptomycin 10-12 mg/kg IV daily (high-dose for VRE)
Linezolid 600 mg IV/PO BID (cytopenias risk)
Nitrofurantoin 100 mg PO BID × 5d (if susceptible)
Fosfomycin 3 g PO every 48h ×3 doses

candidemia.title: Candidemia (non-neutropenic)

regimen.dose_text: Micafungin 100 mg IV daily
Caspofungin 70 mg IV ×1 then 50 mg IV daily
Fluconazole 800 mg IV ×1 then 400 mg IV/PO daily — only if low risk + susceptible (NOT C. glabrata, C. krusei, C. auris)

candidemia.duration: 14 days from first negative blood culture

regimen.dose_text: Micafungin 100 mg IV daily
Liposomal amphoB 5 mg/kg IV daily (echinocandin-resistant)
Voriconazole 6 mg/kg IV q12h ×2, then 4 mg/kg IV q12h
Isavuconazole 372 mg q8h ×6 doses then 372 mg daily
TMP-SMX 15-20 mg/kg/d (TMP component) IV/PO divided q6-8h
+ Prednisone 40 mg PO BID ×5d, then 40 mg daily ×5d, then 20 mg daily ×11d if PaO2 <70 or A-a >35 (adjunctive corticosteroid — not an antifungal)

mssa-ssti.title: MSSA SSTI — Non-purulent cellulitis

regimen.dose_text: Cephalexin 500 mg PO q6h (mild, outpatient)
Dicloxacillin 500 mg PO q6h (mild, outpatient; take on empty stomach)
Cefazolin 1-2 g IV q8h (moderate-severe, hospitalized)
Nafcillin/oxacillin 1-2 g IV q4h (severe, confirmed MSSA)
Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg PO BID (oral alternative with broader coverage)

mssa-ssti.caveat: Streptococcal coverage is primary goal for non-purulent cellulitis. For purulent MSSA (abscess with systemic signs): incision & drainage is primary therapy; add dicloxacillin/cephalexin PO or cefazolin/nafcillin IV if systemic signs present.

vrsa-treatment.title: VRSA / VISA treatment

regimen.dose_text: Linezolid 600 mg IV/PO q12h
Daptomycin 8-12 mg/kg IV q24h (high-dose; check MIC — cross-resistance possible)
Ceftaroline 600 mg IV q8h (if susceptible — beta-lactam with MRSA activity)

vrsa-treatment.source: IDSA MRSA Guidelines 2011; UNMC VRSA guidance 2022
vrsa-treatment.caveat: VRSA (vancomycin MIC ≥16) and VISA (MIC 4-8): no RCT data. ID consultation mandatory. Combination therapy preferred for serious infection. Quinupristin-dalfopristin 7.5 mg/kg IV q8h is a last-resort option for E. faecium (not faecalis).

visa-treatment.title: VISA / hVISA treatment

regimen.dose_text: Daptomycin 8-10 mg/kg IV q24h (preferred; check VISA MIC)
Linezolid 600 mg IV/PO q12h
Ceftaroline 600 mg IV q8h ± daptomycin (synergy combination)

visa-treatment.caveat: Vancomycin may still be used for hVISA if AUC/MIC targets achievable. Use vancomyzer.com for AUC-based dosing. ID consultation strongly recommended.

cons-line.title: CoNS CLABSI (catheter-related bloodstream infection)

regimen.dose_text: Vancomycin AUC-targeted (vancomyzer.com) + catheter removal preferred
Daptomycin 6 mg/kg IV q24h (catheter removed; non-endocarditis)
Linezolid 600 mg IV/PO q12h (if vancomycin/daptomycin not suitable)

cons-line.duration: 5-7 days (catheter removed, uncomplicated); 10-14 days (catheter retained with antibiotic lock therapy)
cons-line.caveat: Most CoNS are methicillin-resistant — do NOT use beta-lactams empirically. Catheter removal strongly preferred (20% recurrence if retained). Rule out contamination (two sets). Echo if persistent bacteremia.

spn-cap.title: S. pneumoniae CAP — Targeted (once susceptibilities known)

regimen.dose_text: Amoxicillin 1 g PO q8h (penicillin-susceptible MIC ≤0.06)
Penicillin G 2-4 MU IV q4-6h (penicillin-susceptible; hospitalized)
Ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV q24h (penicillin-intermediate or resistant; non-meningeal breakpoint ≤1)
Levofloxacin 750 mg IV/PO daily (penicillin-resistant or intolerant)

spn-cap.caveat: Macrolide resistance 30-40% in US — avoid azithromycin as targeted therapy unless susceptibility confirmed. Meningeal breakpoints are stricter (penicillin MIC ≤0.06 for meningitis).

spn-meningitis.title: S. pneumoniae meningitis — Targeted

regimen.dose_text: Penicillin G 4 MU IV q4h (penicillin MIC ≤0.06 — susceptible)
Ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h (penicillin-intermediate/resistant; ceftriaxone MIC ≤0.5)
Vancomycin + ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h (ceftriaxone MIC >1 or susceptibility unknown)

spn-meningitis.caveat: Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV q6h ×4 days — start with or before first antibiotic dose to reduce mortality and hearing loss. Do NOT use dexamethasone with vancomycin (reduces CNS penetration).

gas-nec-fasc.title: GAS necrotizing fasciitis / Streptococcal TSS

regimen.dose_text: Penicillin G 4 MU IV q4h PLUS clindamycin 900 mg IV q8h (combination required)
Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q8-12h PLUS clindamycin 900 mg IV q8h (PCN allergy)

gas-nec-fasc.duration: Until clinically improved/afebrile; typically 10-14+ days guided by surgical findings
gas-nec-fasc.source: IDSA SSTI Guidelines 2014; CDC GAS guidance 2023
gas-nec-fasc.caveat: SURGICAL DEBRIDEMENT IS PRIMARY THERAPY — emergent, aggressive. Clindamycin suppresses exotoxin synthesis (Eagle effect). IVIG 1-2 g/kg may be considered for streptococcal TSS. Do NOT delay surgery for antibiotics.

gbs-treatment.title: Group B Streptococcus (GBS) bacteremia / meningitis

regimen.dose_text: Penicillin G 18-24 MU/day IV continuous or divided q4-6h (adults)
Ampicillin 2 g IV q4h (equivalent to penicillin G)
Ceftriaxone 2 g IV q24h (adults; PCN allergy — non-anaphylactic)
Vancomycin AUC-targeted (PCN anaphylaxis history)

gbs-treatment.duration: Bacteremia: 10-14 days; Meningitis: 14-21 days; Endocarditis: 4-6 weeks. Neonatal bacteremia: 10-14 days; neonatal meningitis: ≥14 days
gbs-treatment.caveat: Neonatal GBS: use ampicillin + gentamicin empirically, then de-escalate to penicillin G monotherapy once GBS confirmed. Adult GBS is serious — endocarditis risk; echo if bacteremia. All strains susceptible to beta-lactams.

vgs-ie.title: Viridans streptococci infective endocarditis

regimen.dose_text: Penicillin G 12-18 MU/day IV continuous or divided q4-6h ×4 weeks (PCN-susceptible MIC ≤0.125)
Ceftriaxone 2 g IV/IM q24h ×4 weeks (equivalent to penicillin G; easier administration)
Vancomycin AUC-targeted ×4-6 weeks (PCN allergy)
Ampicillin 12-20 g/day IV + gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day ×4 weeks (PCN-resistant VGS MIC >0.5)

vgs-ie.duration: 4 weeks native valve; 6 weeks prosthetic valve. 2-week regimen (PCN + gentamicin) for uncomplicated native valve PCN-susceptible
vgs-ie.caveat: PCN-intermediate (MIC 0.125-0.5): PCN or ceftriaxone ×4 weeks + gentamicin ×first 2 weeks. Gentamicin target: peak 3-4 mcg/mL, trough <1 mcg/mL. Echo (TEE preferred) to characterize vegetation.

s-milleri-abscess.title: Streptococcus anginosus group (milleri) abscess

regimen.dose_text: Penicillin G 12-18 MU/day IV divided q4-6h (induction — confirmed susceptible)
Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg PO BID (step-down or mild; covers polymicrobial/anaerobic co-infection)
Ceftriaxone 2 g IV q24h + metronidazole 500 mg IV q8h (if anaerobic co-infection suspected)
Clindamycin 600 mg IV q8h (PCN allergy)

s-milleri-abscess.duration: 4-6 weeks deep-seated abscess (brain, liver, lung); 2-4 weeks simpler abscesses
s-milleri-abscess.caveat: Drainage is critical — antibiotics alone often insufficient. Often polymicrobial with anaerobes. All S. anginosus reliably susceptible to penicillin, amox-clav, ceftriaxone, and vancomycin.

efaecalis-uti.title: Enterococcus faecalis UTI

regimen.dose_text: Nitrofurantoin macrocrystals 100 mg PO q12h ×5-7 days (uncomplicated; susceptible)
Amoxicillin 500 mg PO q8h ×5-7 days (uncomplicated)
Fosfomycin 3 g PO single dose (uncomplicated cystitis)
Ampicillin 2 g IV q6h ×7-14 days (complicated/catheter-associated)

efaecalis-uti.caveat: Enterococcus is intrinsically resistant to cephalosporins — do not use ceftriaxone or cephalexin to treat enterococcal UTI. Confirm susceptibility (ampicillin-susceptible E. faecalis vs. VRE).

efaecalis-ie.title: Enterococcus faecalis infective endocarditis

regimen.dose_text: Ampicillin 2 g IV q4h PLUS ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h ×6 weeks (preferred — less nephrotoxicity than aminoglycoside regimen)
Ampicillin 12-20 g/day IV divided q4h PLUS gentamicin 3 mg/kg/day IV q8h ×4-6 weeks (alternative)
Vancomycin + gentamicin (PCN allergy; VRE: substitute daptomycin for vancomycin)

efaecalis-ie.duration: 4 weeks (symptoms <3 months, native valve); 6 weeks (symptoms >3 months, prosthetic valve, relapse)
efaecalis-ie.caveat: Ampicillin + ceftriaxone increasingly preferred over aminoglycoside regimen (equal efficacy, less nephrotoxicity). Gentamicin target: peak 3-4 mcg/mL, trough <1 mcg/mL. Cephalosporins alone do NOT kill enterococci — they enhance ampicillin binding (saturation of PBPs).

efaecium-treatment.title: Enterococcus faecium (VRE) bacteremia / serious infection

regimen.dose_text: Linezolid 600 mg IV/PO q12h (FDA-approved for VRE)
Daptomycin 8-12 mg/kg IV q24h (high-dose; check MIC — standard 6 mg/kg may be insufficient)
Tigecycline 100 mg IV load then 50 mg IV q12h (bacteriostatic; caution in bacteremia — inferior outcomes)

efaecium-treatment.caveat: E. faecium more resistant than faecalis. Daptomycin combination (+ ampicillin or ceftaroline) may improve bacteremia outcomes. Linezolid toxicity (thrombocytopenia, neuropathy) with prolonged use — monitor CBC. For VRE UTI: nitrofurantoin (if susceptible) or fosfomycin.

listeria-meningitis.title: Listeria monocytogenes meningitis

regimen.dose_text: Ampicillin 2 g IV q4h PLUS gentamicin 1.7 mg/kg IV q8h ×first 7 days (synergistic induction)
Ampicillin 2 g IV q4h monotherapy ×21 days (if gentamicin nephrotoxicity concern)
TMP-SMX 5 mg/kg (trimethoprim component) IV/PO q6h ×21 days (PCN allergy — first alternative)
Meropenem 2 g IV q8h ×21 days (PCN allergy, severe)

listeria-meningitis.caveat: CEPHALOSPORINS HAVE NO LISTERIA ACTIVITY — never use as empiric monotherapy in at-risk patients (>50 years, immunocompromised, pregnant). Dexamethasone NOT recommended for Listeria meningitis (associated with worse outcomes). Must be in empiric coverage for at-risk groups.

coryne-true-infection.title: Corynebacterium true infection (non-JK)

regimen.dose_text: Penicillin G 12-18 MU/day IV divided q4-6h ×10-14 days (susceptible strains)
Ampicillin 2 g IV q4h (alternative; C. pseudodiphtheriticum, C. striatum)
Vancomycin AUC-targeted (C. jeikeium/JK — multidrug-resistant species; vancomycin is DOC)

coryne-true-infection.caveat: C. jeikeium (JK) is highly MDR — vancomycin is drug of choice. For C. diphtheriae: diphtheria antitoxin (DAT) is cornerstone of therapy — request from CDC; erythromycin or penicillin G used to eliminate carriage. Always obtain susceptibilities on clinical isolates.

b-cereus-treatment.title: Bacillus cereus serious infection

regimen.dose_text: Vancomycin AUC-targeted (drug of choice — intrinsic beta-lactam resistance)
Clindamycin 600-900 mg IV q8h
Ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV q12h (alternative for non-CNS infection)
Linezolid 600 mg IV/PO q12h

b-cereus-treatment.duration: 7-14 days for serious infection; clinical guidance
b-cereus-treatment.source: Expert consensus; Medscape B. cereus treatment
b-cereus-treatment.caveat: B. cereus is intrinsically resistant to penicillin, amoxicillin, and most cephalosporins. For food poisoning/gastroenteritis: supportive care only (self-limited). Serious infections (bacteremia, endophthalmitis, meningitis) occur in immunocompromised or IV drug users. Ophthalmology consult mandatory for endophthalmitis.

anthrax-treatment.title: Bacillus anthracis (anthrax)

regimen.dose_text: Ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV q12h (systemic) or 500 mg PO q12h (cutaneous/PEP)
Doxycycline 100 mg IV/PO q12h (cutaneous/PEP alternative; poor CNS penetration — not for meningitis)
Ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV q12h + clindamycin 900 mg IV q8h (inhalational/systemic — combination required; clindamycin suppresses toxin synthesis)

anthrax-treatment.duration: Cutaneous natural acquisition: 7-10 days; bioterrorism PEP: 60 days; inhalational systemic: 60 days total (IV then step to PO)
anthrax-treatment.caveat: Add anthrax antitoxin (obiltoxaximab or raxibacumab) for systemic/inhalational disease. Use cipro not doxy for CNS involvement. Notify public health immediately. Combination therapy for systemic disease. Anthrax vaccine if PEP scenario.

meningococcus-treatment.title: Neisseria meningitidis meningitis

regimen.dose_text: Ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h ×7 days
Penicillin G 4 MU IV q4h ×7 days (confirmed penicillin-susceptible)
Ampicillin 2 g IV q4h (alternative to penicillin G; same spectrum)

meningococcus-treatment.source: IDSA Bacterial Meningitis 2004; CDC meningococcal guidance
meningococcus-treatment.caveat: Notify public health. Close contacts require chemoprophylaxis: ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO single dose (check local FQ resistance) or rifampin 600 mg PO q12h ×2 days. Dexamethasone benefit limited for meningococcal disease but acceptable empirically. Isolation precautions until 24h of antibiotics.

gc-disseminated.title: Disseminated gonococcal infection (DGI)

regimen.dose_text: Ceftriaxone 1 g IV/IM q24h; step-down to oral cefixime 400 mg PO BID once improved and susceptible
Azithromycin 1 g PO/IV daily (alternative if cephalosporin not feasible)

gc-disseminated.duration: Arthritis-dermatitis: ≥7 days total; Meningitis: ≥2 weeks; Endocarditis: ≥4 weeks
gc-disseminated.caveat: Higher ceftriaxone dose (1 g) vs. uncomplicated gonorrhea (500 mg). Always test/treat for chlamydia co-infection. Oral step-down only after improvement and susceptibility confirmed. Test-of-cure recommended.

moraxella-respiratory.title: Moraxella catarrhalis respiratory (AECOPD/sinusitis)

regimen.dose_text: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg PO BID ×5-7 days
Azithromycin 500 mg PO day 1 then 250 mg days 2-5
Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID ×5-7 days
Levofloxacin 750 mg PO daily ×5 days (structural lung disease or recent antibiotic use)

moraxella-respiratory.caveat: Nearly 100% of M. catarrhalis produce beta-lactamase — amoxicillin alone is ineffective. Virtually all strains susceptible to amox-clav, azithromycin, doxycycline, and oral cephalosporins.

hflu-respiratory.title: Haemophilus influenzae respiratory (AECOPD/pneumonia)

regimen.dose_text: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg PO BID ×5-7 days (covers beta-lactamase producers)
Azithromycin 500 mg PO then 250 mg ×4 days
Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID ×5 days
Ampicillin-sulbactam 3 g IV q6h (hospitalized)
Ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV q24h (hospitalized)

hflu-respiratory.caveat: ~40-50% of H. influenzae produce beta-lactamase — use amox-clav empirically unless beta-lactamase negative confirmed. Non-typeable H. influenzae most common in AECOPD. Fluoroquinolones reserved for severe disease or prior antibiotic exposure.

hflu-meningitis.title: Haemophilus influenzae meningitis

regimen.dose_text: Ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h (adults); 100 mg/kg/day IV q12h (children, max 4 g/day)
Ampicillin 2 g IV q4h (adults; only if beta-lactamase NEGATIVE confirmed)
Meropenem 2 g IV q8h (beta-lactam allergy or resistant strains)

hflu-meningitis.caveat: Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV q6h ×4 days — strong evidence for H. flu type b meningitis in children (reduces hearing loss). Do NOT use ampicillin empirically (up to 80% global resistance). Chemoprophylaxis for close contacts: rifampin 20 mg/kg/day (max 600 mg) PO q24h ×4 days.

ecoli-uti.title: E. coli uncomplicated cystitis

regimen.dose_text: Nitrofurantoin macrocrystals 100 mg PO BID ×5 days
TMP-SMX DS (160/800 mg) PO BID ×3 days (if local resistance <20%)
Fosfomycin 3 g PO single dose
Pivmecillinam 400 mg PO BID ×3-5 days (where available)

ecoli-uti.caveat: Fluoroquinolones not first-line for uncomplicated cystitis (stewardship). Avoid nitrofurantoin in complicated UTI (poor tissue penetration). Fosfomycin NOT for pyelonephritis. Check local resistance before using TMP-SMX (aim <20%).

ecoli-pyelo.title: E. coli pyelonephritis

regimen.dose_text: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BID ×7 days (outpatient, susceptible)
Levofloxacin 750 mg PO daily ×5 days (outpatient alternative)
TMP-SMX DS PO BID ×14 days (if susceptible; inferior to FQ)
Ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV q24h (hospitalized)
Ertapenem 1 g IV q24h (ESBL suspected/confirmed)

ecoli-pyelo.duration: Outpatient: 5-7 days (FQ); 14 days (TMP-SMX). Hospitalized: 7-14 days total
ecoli-pyelo.caveat: Avoid nitrofurantoin and fosfomycin (insufficient tissue/serum levels). If ESBL confirmed: prefer carbapenem over pip-tazo (MERINO trial — higher mortality with pip-tazo for ESBL bacteremia). Obtain urine culture before starting.

ecoli-bsi.title: E. coli bacteremia / BSI

regimen.dose_text: Ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV q24h (susceptible strains; source controlled)
Cefazolin 2 g IV q8h (susceptible; uncomplicated source)
Meropenem 1-2 g IV q8h (ESBL-producing E. coli)
Ertapenem 1 g IV q24h (ESBL; stable, source controlled)

ecoli-bsi.duration: 14 days uncomplicated BSI; 4-6 weeks endovascular infection
ecoli-bsi.source: IDSA AMR Guidance 2022; Stanford GNR guide 2025
ecoli-bsi.caveat: Source control essential. Pip-tazo NOT recommended for ESBL-E bacteremia (MERINO trial). Oral step-down to ciprofloxacin or TMP-SMX feasible in stable patients. Follow-up blood cultures at 48-72h.

kpn-treatment.title: Klebsiella pneumoniae (susceptible) treatment

regimen.dose_text: Ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV q24h (bacteremia, pneumonia)
Cefazolin 2 g IV q8h (susceptible bacteremia, step-down candidate)
Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV q6h (broad-spectrum empiric)
Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BID (oral step-down; if susceptible)

kpn-treatment.duration: UTI: 7-14 days; Bacteremia/CAP: 7-14 days; Liver abscess: 4-6 weeks
kpn-treatment.caveat: K. pneumoniae intrinsically resistant to ampicillin. Always check for ESBL or KPC production — if present, treat as resistant (see IDSA AMR guidance). KPC-producing K. pneumoniae: ceftazidime-avibactam preferred.

ampc-treatment.title: AmpC-producing Enterobacteriaceae (Enterobacter, Citrobacter freundii, Serratia, Morganella, Providencia)

regimen.dose_text: Cefepime 2 g IV q8h (preferred for Enterobacter cloacae, K. aerogenes, C. freundii — AmpC-stable)
Meropenem 1-2 g IV q8h (invasive infections; Enterobacter with high inoculum)
Ertapenem 1 g IV q24h (stable, non-Pseudomonas infections)
Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BID / 400 mg IV q12h (oral step-down; if susceptible — Serratia, Morganella)

ampc-treatment.duration: Bacteremia 14 days; UTI 5-7 days; Pneumonia 7 days
ampc-treatment.caveat: DO NOT use ceftriaxone or 3rd-gen cephalosporins for invasive E. cloacae, C. freundii, or K. aerogenes — AmpC derepression causes clinical treatment failure even when initially susceptible in vitro. Pip-tazo also NOT recommended for serious AmpC infections.

proteus-uti.title: Proteus mirabilis UTI

regimen.dose_text: TMP-SMX DS PO BID ×3 days (women, uncomplicated) or ×7 days (men, complicated)
Ciprofloxacin 250 mg PO BID ×3 days (women) or 500 mg BID ×7-14 days (complicated)
Ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV q24h (hospitalized; pyelonephritis)
Ampicillin-sulbactam 3 g IV q6h (if susceptible)

proteus-uti.source: IDSA UTI Guidelines 2010/2025; Hopkins ABX Guide
proteus-uti.caveat: P. mirabilis intrinsically RESISTANT to nitrofurantoin and colistin — do NOT use. Produces urease → alkaline urine → struvite stones; complete treatment of stone-forming UTIs. Ampicillin resistance variable (10-20%).

nts-treatment.title: Salmonella non-typhi (NTS) gastroenteritis and bacteremia

regimen.dose_text: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BID ×5-7 days (bacteremia/high-risk; check FQ susceptibility)
Ceftriaxone 2 g IV q24h ×7-14 days (bacteremia; preferred over FQ if resistance suspected)
Azithromycin 500 mg PO daily ×3-5 days (FQ-resistant NTS gastroenteritis)

nts-treatment.duration: Gastroenteritis (when treated): 3-5 days; Bacteremia: 10-14 days; Focal infection (osteo): 4-6 weeks
nts-treatment.caveat: Antibiotics generally NOT indicated for uncomplicated immunocompetent NTS gastroenteritis (may prolong carriage). Treat if: age <12 months or >65 years, severe illness, HIV/immunocompromised, prosthetic hardware, hemoglobinopathy, sickle cell disease. Fluoroquinolone resistance increasing globally.

typhoid-treatment.title: Salmonella typhi — typhoid fever

regimen.dose_text: Azithromycin 1 g PO daily ×5-7 days (uncomplicated; preferred empiric — avoids FQ resistance)
Ceftriaxone 2 g IV q24h ×10-14 days (complicated/severe; parenteral)
Ciprofloxacin 500-750 mg PO BID or 400 mg IV q12h ×7-10 days (FQ-susceptible confirmed, non-travel from Pakistan/Iraq)

typhoid-treatment.duration: Uncomplicated azithromycin: 5-7 days; IV ceftriaxone: 10-14 days
typhoid-treatment.source: CDC Typhoid Fever Clinical Guidance 2023; AAFP 2021
typhoid-treatment.caveat: Most US typhoid acquired abroad (South Asia). Do NOT use FQ empirically for travel from Pakistan/Iraq (XDR strains resistant to: amp, chloramphenicol, TMP-SMX, cipro, 3rd-gen cephalosporins). XDR: azithromycin ± meropenem. Test-of-cure stool culture recommended.

shigella-treatment.title: Shigella treatment

regimen.dose_text: Azithromycin 500 mg PO daily ×3 days (preferred empiric — increasing FQ resistance)
Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BID ×3-5 days (if susceptibility confirmed)
Ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV q24h ×3-5 days (severe; children hospitalized)

shigella-treatment.source: IDSA Infectious Diarrhea 2017; CDC Shigella resistance alert 2023
shigella-treatment.caveat: Treat: S. dysenteriae type 1 (all cases), immunocompromised, HCW, food handlers, or severe illness. FQ AND macrolide resistance increasing — check susceptibilities. Avoid antimotility agents. Report to public health.

campy-treatment.title: Campylobacter treatment

regimen.dose_text: Azithromycin 500 mg PO daily ×3 days (preferred — ~47% FQ resistance in US)
Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BID ×3-5 days (only if susceptibility confirmed)

campy-treatment.duration: 3-5 days; 7+ days for bacteremia or immunocompromised
campy-treatment.caveat: Most Campylobacter is self-limited in immunocompetent hosts — antibiotics often not required. FQ resistance ~47% in US — azithromycin preferred empirically. Guillain-Barré syndrome is a rare post-infectious sequela.

yersinia-treatment.title: Yersinia enterocolitica (serious infection)

regimen.dose_text: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO BID or 400 mg IV q12h ×7-10 days
TMP-SMX DS PO BID ×7-10 days (alternative)
Ceftriaxone 2 g IV q24h (bacteremia; severe)
Doxycycline 100 mg PO/IV BID (alternative)

yersinia-treatment.caveat: Antibiotics generally NOT recommended for uncomplicated Yersinia gastroenteritis in immunocompetent hosts (self-limited). Treat: bacteremia, immunocompromised, or mesenteric lymphadenitis with sepsis. Y. enterocolitica intrinsically resistant to ampicillin and 1st-gen cephalosporins.

bcc-treatment.title: Burkholderia cepacia complex (CF exacerbation)

regimen.dose_text: Meropenem 2 g IV q8h PLUS TMP-SMX 15-20 mg/kg/day (trimethoprim) IV divided q6-8h — combination required
Ceftazidime 2 g IV q8h (if susceptible; alternative to meropenem)
Minocycline 100 mg IV/PO BID (combination component or alternative)

bcc-treatment.caveat: Intrinsically resistant to aminoglycosides, polymyxins, and many beta-lactams. Combination therapy required — synergy between TMP-SMX and carbapenems. Susceptibility testing essential (≥18 genomovars with variable resistance). Cepacia syndrome (necrotizing pneumonia) has high mortality. ID consultation mandatory.

vibrio-treatment.title: Vibrio vulnificus infection

regimen.dose_text: Doxycycline 100 mg IV/PO BID PLUS ceftriaxone 2 g IV q24h (combination — preferred)
Doxycycline 100 mg IV/PO BID PLUS cefepime 2 g IV q12h (alternative combination for severe cases)
Ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV q12h (with doxycycline; alternative combination partner)

vibrio-treatment.caveat: Medical emergency — mortality >50% with septicemia. Start treatment IMMEDIATELY if raw shellfish exposure (especially oysters) + septic shock or wound infection after seawater. Aggressive surgical debridement for necrotizing wound infections — do not delay.

legionella-treatment.title: Legionella pneumophila pneumonia (targeted)

regimen.dose_text: Levofloxacin 750 mg IV/PO q24h ×7-14 days (severe or hospitalized; preferred)
Azithromycin 500 mg IV/PO q24h ×7-14 days (mild-moderate)
Moxifloxacin 400 mg IV/PO q24h (FQ alternative)
Doxycycline 100 mg IV/PO BID (alternative; second-line)

legionella-treatment.duration: Immunocompetent: 7-10 days (mild) to 10-14 days (severe); Immunocompromised: 14-21 days
legionella-treatment.caveat: Fluoroquinolones preferred for severe disease (faster defervescence, shorter LOS vs. macrolides). Beta-lactams ineffective (intracellular pathogen with intrinsic beta-lactamase). Excellent intracellular concentrations achieved with FQ and macrolides. IV-to-PO conversion without dose change for levofloxacin. Notify health department.

mycoplasma-treatment.title: Mycoplasma pneumoniae / Chlamydophila pneumoniae (targeted)

regimen.dose_text: Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID ×5-7 days (adults; preferred — coverage for macrolide-resistant Mycoplasma)
Azithromycin 500 mg PO day 1 then 250 mg days 2-5 (outpatient; macrolide-susceptible)
Levofloxacin 750 mg IV/PO daily (hospitalized; or macrolide/doxy intolerant)
Moxifloxacin 400 mg IV/PO daily (hospitalized alternative)

mycoplasma-treatment.caveat: Beta-lactams have NO activity (no cell wall). Macrolide resistance ~30% in Asia (less common in US currently). If no improvement on azithromycin within 48-72h, switch to doxycycline or fluoroquinolone. Tetracyclines and fluoroquinolones preferred for adults.

lyme-treatment.title: Borrelia burgdorferi — Lyme disease

regimen.dose_text: Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID ×10 days (early localized EM); ×14-21 days (early disseminated); ×28 days (Lyme arthritis)
Amoxicillin 500 mg PO TID ×14 days (pregnancy, children <8y; early localized/disseminated)
Ceftriaxone 2 g IV q24h ×14-21 days (neurological Lyme, Lyme meningitis, refractory arthritis, cardiac with AV block)

lyme-treatment.duration: 10 days early localized; 14-21 days disseminated/neuro; 28 days arthritis
lyme-treatment.caveat: Post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome (PTLDS): prolonged antibiotics NOT recommended. Azithromycin less effective — use only if other agents contraindicated. Do NOT use chronic antibiotic therapy for persistent symptoms. Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction may occur in first 24h.

oral-anaerobes-treatment.title: Oral anaerobes / aspiration pneumonia

regimen.dose_text: Ampicillin-sulbactam 3 g IV q6h (hospitalized; covers oral anaerobes + streptococci)
Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg PO BID ×5-7 days (outpatient; classic aspiration with gingival disease)
Ceftriaxone 2 g IV daily + metronidazole 500 mg IV q8h (lung abscess or empyema present)
Clindamycin 300-450 mg PO TID ×5-7 days (outpatient PCN allergy; note C. diff risk)

oral-anaerobes-treatment.duration: 5-7 days uncomplicated aspiration pneumonia; 14-28 days lung abscess
oral-anaerobes-treatment.caveat: Per IDSA 2019: do NOT add routine anaerobic coverage for aspiration pneumonia unless lung abscess, empyema, or history of witnessed aspiration with gingival disease is present. Clindamycin monotherapy not routinely recommended (C. diff risk, limited evidence).

bacteroides-treatment.title: Bacteroides fragilis / intraabdominal anaerobic infection

regimen.dose_text: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV q6h (moderate-severe, hospital-acquired; monotherapy)
Meropenem 1 g IV q8h (high-severity; monotherapy)
Ceftriaxone 2 g IV q24h + metronidazole 500 mg IV q8h (mild-moderate community-acquired)
Ertapenem 1 g IV q24h (mild-moderate; convenient monotherapy)
Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg PO BID (oral step-down)

bacteroides-treatment.duration: 4-7 days source-controlled; up to 14 days if inadequate source control
bacteroides-treatment.caveat: Source control (drainage, surgery) is essential — antibiotics alone insufficient. Metronidazole NOT needed with pip-tazo or carbapenems (already cover anaerobes). Metronidazole IS needed with ceftazidime/cefepime (cephalosporins lack B. fragilis coverage). Clindamycin resistance increasing (20-30%).

c-perfringens-treatment.title: Clostridium perfringens — gas gangrene / clostridial myonecrosis

regimen.dose_text: Penicillin G 4 MU IV q4h (24 MU/day) PLUS clindamycin 900 mg IV q8h (combination required)
Clindamycin 900 mg IV q8h + metronidazole 500 mg IV q8h (PCN allergy)
Meropenem 1-2 g IV q8h + clindamycin 900 mg IV q8h (carbapenem + toxin suppressor; severe PCN allergy)

c-perfringens-treatment.duration: Until clinically resolved; typically 10-14+ days guided by surgery
c-perfringens-treatment.caveat: SURGICAL DEBRIDEMENT is primary and life-saving treatment — emergent. Clindamycin suppresses toxin synthesis (alpha-toxin). Hyperbaric oxygen NOT recommended (delays surgery, unproven). C. perfringens does NOT produce beta-lactamase — penicillin G fully active.

actinomyces-treatment.title: Actinomyces treatment

regimen.dose_text: Penicillin G 18-24 MU/day IV divided q4-6h ×2-6 weeks (induction until improvement)
Amoxicillin 500-750 mg PO TID (step-down/maintenance; complete 6-12 months total)
Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg PO BID (step-down; covers polymicrobial co-infection)
Doxycycline 100 mg PO/IV BID ×6-12 months (PCN allergy)

actinomyces-treatment.caveat: Actinomyces is a gram-positive anaerobic rod — extremely susceptible to penicillin. Surgery often needed for bulky disease. Often co-infects with companion bacteria (Actinobacillus, Fusobacterium) — may need broader initial coverage. Short-course (<6 months) may be successful for cervicofacial disease if surgically addressed.

nocardia-treatment.title: Nocardia treatment

regimen.dose_text: TMP-SMX 1-2 DS tabs (160/800 mg each) PO BID — mild-moderate pulmonary/SSTI
Imipenem 500 mg IV q6h PLUS amikacin 7.5 mg/kg IV q12h (severe/disseminated/CNS — induction 2-6 weeks; may add TMP-SMX as 3rd agent)
Linezolid 600 mg IV/PO BID (sulfa intolerant; active against virtually all pathogenic Nocardia)

nocardia-treatment.duration: Pulmonary (immunocompetent): 6-12 months; Disseminated/CNS: 12 months; Cutaneous: 3-4 months
nocardia-treatment.caveat: Species-specific susceptibility varies significantly (N. farcinica most resistant). AST should guide therapy. TMP-SMX monotherapy inadequate for CNS or disseminated disease. Neurosurgical drainage for brain abscess often required.

tb-treatment.title: Tuberculosis (active pulmonary) — RIPE regimen

regimen.dose_text: Isoniazid 5 mg/kg (max 300 mg) + rifampin 10 mg/kg (max 600 mg) + pyrazinamide 20-25 mg/kg (max 2 g) + ethambutol 15-20 mg/kg (max 1.6 g) — all PO daily ×2 months (intensive phase)
Isoniazid + rifampin (continuation phase ×4 months after RIPE — 6 months total)

tb-treatment.duration: 6 months total (2 months RIPE + 4 months INH+RIF); extend to 9 months for cavitary disease with positive culture at month 2
tb-treatment.caveat: Always add pyridoxine (B6) 25-50 mg/day to prevent INH neuropathy. Directly Observed Therapy (DOT) strongly recommended. Ethambutol can be stopped if fully susceptible to INH+RIF+PZA. Monitor: LFTs (monthly), vision/color (ethambutol), uric acid (PZA). Drug-susceptibility testing mandatory. HIV co-infection: complex rifamycin-ART interactions — ID/HIV specialist required.

mac-pulmonary.title: NTM / MAC pulmonary disease

regimen.dose_text: Azithromycin 250-500 mg PO 3×/week (M/W/F) PLUS rifampin 600 mg PO 3×/week PLUS ethambutol 25 mg/kg 3×/week (nodular/bronchiectatic disease — 3×/week intermittent regimen)
Azithromycin 250-500 mg PO daily PLUS rifampin 600 mg PO daily PLUS ethambutol 15 mg/kg PO daily (fibrocavitary disease — daily regimen required)
Clarithromycin 500 mg PO BID (3×/week for nodular; daily for cavitary) PLUS rifabutin 300 mg PO daily PLUS ethambutol 15-25 mg/kg (alternative if azithromycin not tolerated)

mac-pulmonary.duration: 12 months after culture conversion (sputum negativity); often 18-24 months total
mac-pulmonary.caveat: Treat only when meeting all three criteria: (1) positive sputum culture ≥2 times or 1 BAL culture, (2) radiographic evidence of disease, (3) symptoms with no other explanation. Macrolide MONOTHERAPY forbidden (rapid resistance). Consider amikacin IV or ALIS (Arikayce) for severe fibrocavitary disease.

crypto-meningitis.title: Cryptococcal meningitis

regimen.dose_text: Liposomal amphotericin B 3-4 mg/kg/day IV PLUS flucytosine 25 mg/kg PO q6h ×14 days (induction — IDSA preferred)
Amphotericin B deoxycholate 0.7-1 mg/kg/day IV PLUS flucytosine 25 mg/kg PO q6h ×14 days (induction — if liposomal not available)
Fluconazole 400 mg PO daily ×8 weeks (consolidation after induction)
Fluconazole 200 mg PO daily (maintenance — ≥1 year; lifelong if HIV without immune reconstitution)

crypto-meningitis.duration: Induction: 14 days; Consolidation: 8 weeks; Maintenance: ≥12 months (lifelong in HIV without ART response)
crypto-meningitis.caveat: Monitor opening pressure at each LP — therapeutic LP daily if >25 cmH2O. Flucytosine: renal-dose-adjust, monitor bone marrow weekly. Avoid mannitol and steroids (no benefit; steroids harmful in HIV-negative crypto). IRIS can occur post-ART initiation in HIV.

histo-treatment.title: Histoplasma capsulatum treatment

regimen.dose_text: Itraconazole 200 mg PO TID ×3 days (loading), then 200 mg PO BID ×12 weeks (mild-moderate pulmonary or disseminated)
Liposomal amphotericin B 3 mg/kg/day IV ×1-2 weeks, then itraconazole step-down ×≥12 months (moderately severe to severe or disseminated)

histo-treatment.duration: Mild pulmonary: 12 weeks; Disseminated: ≥12 months; CNS: ≥12 months after CSF normalizes
histo-treatment.caveat: Itraconazole TDM required — target serum level (itra + OH-itra) >1 mcg/mL. Capsule vs. solution bioavailability differs (solution preferred). Liposomal AmB preferred over deoxycholate when available. CNS histoplasmosis: L-AmB 5 mg/kg/day ×4-6 weeks then itraconazole ≥12 months.

cocci-treatment.title: Coccidioides (Valley Fever) treatment

regimen.dose_text: Fluconazole 400 mg PO daily ×3-6 months (symptomatic pulmonary, moderate)
Itraconazole 200 mg PO BID ×3-6 months (alternative to fluconazole for bone/joint disease)
Fluconazole 400-1200 mg PO/IV daily (LIFELONG — coccidioidal meningitis is never cured without lifelong azole)
Liposomal amphotericin B 3-5 mg/kg/day IV (severe/rapidly progressive until stabilized, then transition to azole)

cocci-treatment.duration: Pulmonary: 3-6 months; Disseminated bone/soft tissue: individualized; Meningitis: LIFELONG
cocci-treatment.caveat: Mild/asymptomatic primary pulmonary coccidioidomycosis may not require treatment. Meningitis: LIFELONG azole therapy (disease is never cured). Pregnant women with disseminated disease: use AmB (azoles teratogenic in pregnancy). Fluconazole <400 mg/day not effective for meningitis.

blasto-treatment.title: Blastomyces dermatitidis treatment

regimen.dose_text: Itraconazole 200 mg PO TID ×3 days (loading), then 200 mg PO BID ×6-12 months (mild-moderate)
Liposomal amphotericin B 3-5 mg/kg/day IV ×1-2 weeks, then itraconazole step-down ×6-12 months total (moderately severe to severe/ARDS)
Fluconazole 800 mg PO daily (CNS blastomycosis maintenance — better CNS penetration than itraconazole; after AmB induction ≥4-6 weeks)

blasto-treatment.caveat: Itraconazole TDM required. Early L-AmB essential for severe pulmonary blastomycosis (can cause ARDS). CNS disease: use fluconazole (not itraconazole) for maintenance due to better CNS penetration. Voriconazole is off-label alternative.

mucor-treatment.title: Mucormycosis treatment

regimen.dose_text: Liposomal amphotericin B 5-10 mg/kg/day IV (start at 5 mg/kg; increase to 10 mg/kg for CNS/rhinocerebral or post-transplant)
Isavuconazole 200 mg (as 372 mg isavuconazonium) IV q8h ×2 days (loading), then 200 mg IV/PO daily (step-down or when AmB not tolerated)
Posaconazole DR tablet 300 mg PO BID ×1 day then 300 mg PO daily (step-down from AmB; salvage)

mucor-treatment.duration: Until clinical/radiographic resolution — often months; no fixed endpoint
mucor-treatment.caveat: SURGICAL DEBRIDEMENT is cornerstone — remove all necrotic tissue. Reverse predisposing factors: control hyperglycemia, reduce immunosuppression. VORICONAZOLE HAS NO ACTIVITY against Mucor — do not use. Isavuconazole preferred over AmB when renal compromise present. ID + surgical + ENT/ophthalmology coordination essential.

hsv-treatment.title: Herpes simplex virus (HSV) treatment

regimen.dose_text: Valacyclovir 1 g PO BID ×7-10 days (primary genital HSV); 500 mg PO BID ×3 days (recurrent); 500-1000 mg PO daily (suppression)
Acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8h ×21 days (HSV encephalitis — IV formulation required; do NOT use valacyclovir for encephalitis)
Acyclovir 20 mg/kg IV q8h ×14-21 days (neonatal HSV — 21 days for CNS/disseminated; 14 days for SEM)

hsv-treatment.duration: Primary mucocutaneous: 7-10 days; Encephalitis: 21 days; Neonatal: 14-21 days
hsv-treatment.caveat: HSV encephalitis: start IV acyclovir empirically — do not wait for CSF PCR. Full 21-day course even with improvement (shorter courses associated with relapse). Acyclovir-resistant HSV: foscarnet 40 mg/kg IV q8h. Renal dose-adjust IV acyclovir (nephrotoxicity); prehydrate.

vzv-treatment.title: Varicella-zoster virus (VZV) / Herpes zoster treatment

regimen.dose_text: Valacyclovir 1 g PO TID ×7 days (preferred — shingles immunocompetent adult; start within 72h of rash)
Acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8h ×7-10 days (disseminated VZV or severe immunocompromised; step down to oral when improving)

vzv-treatment.source: Medscape VZV; NIH HIV OI Guidelines; AAFP 2017
vzv-treatment.caveat: Start within 72h of rash onset for maximum benefit (or any time if new lesions appearing). Immunocompromised: IV acyclovir for visceral, CNS, or disseminated disease. Consider prednisone 60 mg taper for severe pain or ophthalmic zoster (reduces PHN risk) in immunocompetent. Foscarnet for acyclovir-resistant VZV.

cmv-treatment.title: Cytomegalovirus (CMV) treatment

regimen.dose_text: Valganciclovir 900 mg PO BID ×21 days (induction); then 900 mg PO daily (maintenance) — non-severe CMV disease, tolerating oral
Ganciclovir 5 mg/kg IV q12h ×21 days (induction — CMV retinitis immediate sight-threat; colitis; pneumonitis)
IV ganciclovir 5 mg/kg q12h until clinical improvement, then step-down to valganciclovir 900 mg PO daily (CMV colitis/esophagitis)

cmv-treatment.duration: Induction: 14-21 days; Maintenance: until immune reconstitution or per clinical response
cmv-treatment.source: NIH/CDC/IDSA HIV OI Guidelines; AAO CMV retinitis
cmv-treatment.caveat: Monitor CBC weekly (ganciclovir causes neutropenia/thrombocytopenia). Valganciclovir = oral prodrug; preferred over IV for non-severe disease. Renal dose-adjust both agents. Ganciclovir-resistant CMV: foscarnet 90-120 mg/kg IV q12h (nephrotoxic — monitor renal function and electrolytes closely).

flu-treatment.title: Influenza treatment

regimen.dose_text: Oseltamivir 75 mg PO BID ×5 days (outpatient and hospitalized; preferred in pregnancy)
Baloxavir marboxil 40 mg PO single dose if <80 kg; 80 mg if ≥80 kg (outpatient only; equivalent to 5-day oseltamivir)

flu-treatment.duration: 5 days standard; extend in immunocompromised or severe illness
flu-treatment.source: IDSA Influenza Guidelines 2018; CDC Influenza Antivirals 2024-25
flu-treatment.caveat: Start within 48h of symptom onset for greatest benefit; start regardless of timing in hospitalized, high-risk, or severe patients. Oseltamivir preferred in pregnancy, children, and hospitalized. Baloxavir: check antacid interactions. IV peramivir 600 mg single dose available for IV-only route (hospitalized).

rsv-treatment.title: RSV treatment / prophylaxis

regimen.dose_text: Supportive care is the primary treatment. No FDA-approved oral antiviral for immunocompetent patients. Aerosolized ribavirin (off-label): reserved for severe disease in highly immunocompromised (SCT pre-engraftment) — limited evidence.

rsv-treatment.duration: Supportive care duration by clinical course
rsv-treatment.source: AAP RSV Recommendations 2025; CDC RSV Immunization Guidance 2023
rsv-treatment.caveat: PROPHYLAXIS (not treatment): Nirsevimab (Beyfortus) 50 mg IM (<5 kg) or 100 mg IM (≥5 kg) — single dose per RSV season for infants <8 months. Replaced palivizumab (AAP 2025). Adult vaccines (RSVpreF, Arexvy, mRESVIA) available for adults ≥60 years. No antiviral treatment recommended for immunocompetent patients.

covid-treatment.title: SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19) treatment

regimen.dose_text: Nirmatrelvir 300 mg + ritonavir 100 mg PO BID ×5 days (start within 5 days of symptom onset; first-line non-hospitalized high-risk)
Remdesivir 200 mg IV day 1, then 100 mg IV days 2-3 (3-day outpatient course; or 5-day inpatient course for hospitalized not requiring O2 or on low-flow O2)
Molnupiravir 800 mg PO BID ×5 days (last resort only — inferior efficacy; contraindicated in pregnancy)

covid-treatment.source: IDSA COVID-19 Treatment Guidelines 2024/2025; NIH COVID-19 Treatment Guidelines
covid-treatment.caveat: Paxlovid: extensive CYP3A4 drug interactions via ritonavir — review full drug list before prescribing. Check nirmatrelvir dose for CrCl 30-60 (reduce to 150 mg BID). Monoclonal antibodies largely inactive against current variants. COVID-19 treatment landscape evolves — verify current guidance at covid19treatmentguidelines.nih.gov.

hiv-defer.title: HIV antiretroviral therapy

regimen.dose_text: Refer to AIDSinfo (clinicalinfo.hiv.gov) — ART regimens updated continuously. Current preferred regimens are INSTI-based: bictegravir/TAF/emtricitabine (Biktarvy) or dolutegravir + 2 NRTIs. Resistance testing mandatory before initiating ART.

hiv-defer.caveat: This tool does not provide HIV ART recommendations. Please refer to AIDSinfo (clinicalinfo.hiv.gov) for current regimens, resistance profiles, and drug interactions. ID/HIV specialist consultation required.

hpylori-treatment.title: Helicobacter pylori eradication

regimen.dose_text: Bismuth quadruple therapy (BQT) ×14 days — PREFERRED: PPI BID + bismuth 120-300 mg QID + tetracycline 500 mg QID + metronidazole 500 mg TID-QID (do NOT substitute doxycycline for tetracycline)
Concomitant quadruple ×14 days: amoxicillin 1 g BID + clarithromycin 500 mg BID + metronidazole 500 mg BID + PPI BID (only if local clarithromycin resistance <15%)
Rifabutin triple ×14 days: amoxicillin 1 g BID + rifabutin 150 mg BID + PPI BID (alternative first-line; especially if prior macrolide exposure)

hpylori-treatment.caveat: ACG 2024 recommends AGAINST empiric clarithromycin triple therapy (clarithromycin resistance >15% in US). Confirm eradication 4 weeks after completion (urea breath test or stool antigen — NOT serology). Tetracycline (not doxycycline) in BQT regimen. Vonoprazan-based regimens may be used where available.

scabies-treatment.title: Scabies treatment

regimen.dose_text: Permethrin 5% cream — apply from neck to soles; wash off after 8-14 hours; repeat in 7-14 days (2 applications total)
Ivermectin 200 mcg/kg PO single dose with food; repeat in 7-14 days (classic scabies)
Ivermectin 200 mcg/kg PO days 1, 2, 8, 9, 15 PLUS permethrin 5% cream daily ×7 days then 2×/week (crusted/Norwegian scabies — combination mandatory)

scabies-treatment.duration: 2 applications/doses for classic; weeks of combined therapy for crusted scabies
scabies-treatment.source: CDC STI Treatment Guidelines Ectoparasites 2021; PMC11589009 2024
scabies-treatment.caveat: Treat ALL household and close contacts simultaneously. Wash all bedding/clothing in hot water. Ivermectin NOT approved <15 kg or in pregnancy — use permethrin. Crusted scabies is highly contagious; isolation precautions in healthcare settings.

giardia-treatment.title: Giardia lamblia treatment

regimen.dose_text: Tinidazole 2 g PO single dose (preferred — higher cure rate, simpler dosing)
Metronidazole 250 mg PO TID ×5-7 days
Nitazoxanide 500 mg PO BID ×3 days (adults; 200 mg BID ×3 days for children 4-11 years)
Albendazole 400 mg PO daily ×5 days (alternative)

giardia-treatment.source: IDSA Infectious Diarrhea 2017; CDC Giardia clinical care
giardia-treatment.caveat: Avoid alcohol with metronidazole/tinidazole (disulfiram-like reaction). Test-of-cure only in symptomatic patients. Pregnancy: paromomycin 25-35 mg/kg/day divided TID ×7 days (non-absorbable; safe).

malaria-treatment.title: Malaria treatment

regimen.dose_text: Artemether-lumefantrine (Coartem) ≥35 kg: 4 tabs PO at hours 0, 8, 24, 36, 48, 60 with food — uncomplicated P. falciparum (or unknown species, chloroquine-resistant areas)
Atovaquone-proguanil (Malarone) 4 adult tabs PO daily ×3 days with food — uncomplicated P. falciparum alternative
Chloroquine phosphate 1 g PO then 500 mg at 6h, 24h, 48h — P. vivax/ovale/malariae/knowlesi in chloroquine-sensitive areas
Chloroquine (as above) PLUS primaquine 30 mg base PO daily ×14 days after checking G6PD — radical cure for P. vivax/ovale (kills liver hypnozoites)

malaria-treatment.duration: Uncomplicated: 3 days (artemether-lumefantrine, atovaquone-proguanil); Chloroquine: over 48h; Primaquine: 14 days additional
malaria-treatment.caveat: CHECK G6PD before primaquine. Severe/complicated malaria: IV artesunate (contact CDC Malaria Branch 770-488-7788 if unavailable). Chloroquine-resistant P. falciparum now near-universal — do NOT use chloroquine for falciparum. QTc monitoring with chloroquine/quinine at high doses.

toxo-treatment.title: Toxoplasma gondii (CNS/disseminated) treatment

regimen.dose_text: Pyrimethamine 200 mg PO load ×1, then 50-75 mg PO daily PLUS sulfadiazine 1-1.5 g PO q6h PLUS leucovorin 10-25 mg PO daily ×6 weeks (acute therapy)
Pyrimethamine 200 mg load then 50 mg daily + clindamycin 600 mg IV/PO q6h + leucovorin 25 mg daily ×6 weeks (sulfa-intolerant)
TMP-SMX 5 mg/kg (trimethoprim component) IV/PO BID ×6 weeks (if pyrimethamine unavailable — similar efficacy, better tolerated)

toxo-treatment.duration: Acute: 6 weeks; Maintenance: lifelong in HIV until CD4 >200 ×6 months on ART
toxo-treatment.source: NIH/CDC/IDSA HIV OI Guidelines (AIDSinfo); CDC Toxoplasmosis
toxo-treatment.caveat: Leucovorin (folinic acid) MUST be co-administered with pyrimethamine — prevents bone marrow suppression. Monitor CBC weekly during acute therapy. Steroids for CNS edema (>1 cm midline shift). Discontinue maintenance when CD4 >200 for >6 months on effective ART.
